feed_card.headline: The largest living thing on Earth is a fungus in Oregon, one clone spread across 2,385 acres.
teasers: You could walk across it and never know
How you prove a forest hides one creature
It got that big by killing trees

sections.content: The largest living thing on Earth is a fungus in Oregon, one clone spread across 2,385 acres.

content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 400 300' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><text x='200' y='34' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='14' font-weight='600' opacity='0.75' text-anchor='middle'>What you see, and what is really there</text><line x1='20' y1='150' x2='380' y2='150' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.5' stroke-linecap='round'/><g stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.6' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round'><line x1='90' y1='150' x2='90' y2='102'/><circle cx='90' cy='84' r='20'/><line x1='310' y1='150' x2='310' y2='102'/><circle cx='310' cy='84' r='20'/><line x1='200' y1='150' x2='200' y2='92'/><path d='M200 104 l-16 -12 M200 112 l16 -14 M200 96 l-12 -12'/></g><g stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.7' stroke-linecap='round'><line x1='140' y1='150' x2='140' y2='139'/><path d='M131 139 a9 4 0 0 1 18 0'/><line x1='255' y1='150' x2='255' y2='139'/><path d='M246 139 a9 4 0 0 1 18 0'/></g><g stroke='var(--accent)' stroke-width='2.5' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round'><path d='M40 186 C 110 176, 160 206, 214 188 S 322 200, 372 184'/><path d='M90 150 C 92 166, 116 178, 128 184'/><path d='M200 150 C 200 172, 226 182, 236 186'/><path d='M310 150 C 308 166, 286 182, 276 187'/><path d='M140 150 l-2 34 M255 150 l4 32'/><path d='M70 188 l-18 22 M170 194 l14 24 M330 190 l20 22'/></g><text x='200' y='262' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='14' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>One organism, spread underground</text></svg>

sections.content: We can point to the largest living thing on Earth. Almost no one has ever seen it. It is not a whale or a redwood but a fungus, and the part above ground is a scatter of ordinary honey mushrooms that come and go with the rain. The organism itself is the web beneath them: a single clone of Armillaria threading through the soil of an Oregon forest across 2,385 acres. Stand in the middle of it and there is nothing obvious to see. You would be on the largest known individual alive, and it would look like dirt and trees.

items: The single clone covers 3.7 square miles, close to 3 Central Parks laid side by side.
It has been creeping outward for thousands of years, by the high estimate as long as 8,650.
Almost all of it is thread-thin mycelium and black, bootlace-like cords called rhizomorphs, hidden in the soil.
content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 400 300' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><rect x='96' y='48' width='214' height='214' rx='14' stroke='var(--accent)' stroke-width='2.5' fill='var(--accent)' fill-opacity='0.08'/><rect x='96' y='135' width='127' height='127' rx='12' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.6'/><text x='203' y='36' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='15' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>The fungus: 2,385 acres</text><text x='159' y='200' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' font-weight='600' text-anchor='middle' opacity='0.85'>Central Park</text><text x='159' y='218' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-mono), ui-monospace, monospace' font-size='13' text-anchor='middle' opacity='0.7'>843 acres</text></svg>

sections.content: Proving that 2,385 acres of separate mushrooms and dying trees are one creature and not thousands takes lab work. You dig up samples from across the site and grow them side by side. Cells from the same individual merge without a fight. Cells from two different individuals throw up a dark barrier where they meet. Genetic fingerprints then confirm it: across the whole area, the DNA is identical. Nobody counted rings. The age is a calculation. Measure how fast the fungus spreads outward each year, then divide the distance it has covered. That gives thousands of years, with a wide margin of error.

content.body: Two teams, a decade apart, set this record between them. In 1992 researchers in Michigan sampled a honey fungus across a hardwood forest and proved the scattered growth was one individual, about 1,500 years old. The size was striking, but the real prize was the method: a way to show that separate-looking fungus is a single clone. A decade later, Forest Service researchers carried that method to the Blue Mountains of eastern Oregon, hunting the cause of dying conifers. When they mapped the clones in 2003, one sprawled across 2,385 acres.
content.image_caption: Honey fungus mushrooms at the base of a grand fir it is killing, in the Malheur National Forest.
content.image_attribution: Craig L. Schmitt and Michael L. Tatum, “Armillaria ostoyae, Malheur National Forest”, Public Domain (Wikimedia Commons)

content.title: It grows by killing the forest
content.body: Armillaria ostoyae is a killer of trees. It spreads underground as black, bootlace-like cords that find a tree's roots, slip under the bark, and eat into the living wood. A stressed or drought-weakened conifer dies. The fungus feeds on the dead wood and creeps on to the next tree. Its 2,385 acres are the record of a slow kill that has rolled outward for thousands of years.

content.title: Biggest depends on how you measure
content.body: Largest is a slippery word. By sheer weight, the title goes to Pando, a single quaking aspen in Utah. Its 47,000 trunks share one root system and weigh near 6,000 tons. The largest animal ever, the blue whale, reaches maybe 200 tons, a rounding error beside either. By ground covered, though, this fungus wins.
content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 400 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><text x='24' y='30' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='14' font-weight='600' opacity='0.75' text-anchor='start'>Three ways to be biggest</text><g font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='14' font-weight='700' text-anchor='start'><text x='24' y='80' fill='var(--accent)'>Honey fungus</text><text x='24' y='128' fill='currentColor'>Pando aspen</text><text x='24' y='176' fill='currentColor'>Blue whale</text></g><g font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' text-anchor='start' opacity='0.75'><text x='24' y='98' fill='var(--accent)'>largest area</text><text x='24' y='146' fill='currentColor'>heaviest</text><text x='24' y='194' fill='currentColor'>largest animal</text></g><g font-family='var(--font-mono), ui-monospace, monospace' font-size='14' font-weight='700' text-anchor='end'><text x='376' y='88' fill='var(--accent)'>2,385 acres</text><text x='376' y='136' fill='currentColor'>6,000 tons</text><text x='376' y='184' fill='currentColor'>200 tons</text></g></svg>

sections.content: The strange part is not the size. It is the word “single”. Every mushroom, every cord in the soil across those 2,385 acres carries the same genome, grown from one spore that landed there thousands of years ago. Most of that body is not alive at once. The fungus dies at the center while it advances at the edges, the way a ripple keeps its shape while the water moves through it. It stretches our idea of what one living thing is.

content.question: What earns the Oregon honey fungus the title of largest organism?
options: It is the tallest living thing on Earth
It covers the most ground, a single clone spread over 2,385 acres
It contains more cells than any other organism
It is the oldest living thing ever found
content.explanation: The record is about area. A single genetically identical clone sprawls across 2,385 acres, more ground than any other known organism, even though most of it is thin threads in the soil.

content.question: How did researchers show the 2,385 acres was one individual and not many?
options: They watched it grow from a single spore over the years
They counted all of its mushrooms
Samples from across the area were genetically identical and merged instead of rejecting each other
They found one giant central mushroom feeding the rest
content.explanation: Cells from the same individual fuse, while cells from different individuals form a rejection barrier. Identical genetics across the whole site, plus that merging, means it is one clone.

content.question: The mushrooms visible above ground are best understood as:
options: The whole organism itself
The temporary fruiting bodies of a much larger underground network
Separate organisms that happen to share DNA
The oldest surviving part of the fungus
content.explanation: The mushrooms are just the spore-making parts that pop up and vanish. The organism is the vast mesh of threads and cords hidden in the soil.

content.question: Why is calling it the largest organism more complicated than it first sounds?
options: Because a fungus cannot actually be measured
Because it is really made of thousands of separate organisms
Because Pando the aspen is heavier and the blue whale is the largest animal, so the record depends on the measure
Because the fungus is steadily shrinking
content.explanation: Largest by area, heaviest by weight, and largest animal are three different records held by three different organisms. Which one is the biggest depends on what you measure.

content.question: How does the fungus spread across a forest?
options: Its mushrooms migrate to new ground
Wind carries the whole organism to new sites
It only appears where trees have already died naturally
It infects and kills trees, creeping underground by rootlike cords
content.explanation: It is a pathogen. Bootlace-like rhizomorphs reach a tree's roots, kill it, feed on the dead wood, and push on, so growth and killing are the same act.

content.question: Why is the fungus's age, put as high as 8,650 years, so uncertain?
options: It is calculated from how fast the fungus spreads, not counted directly
Fungi cannot be dated by any method
The original records were lost
Carbon dating does not work in forest soil
content.explanation: There are no rings to count. Researchers divide how far it has spread by its yearly growth rate, which gives a figure in the thousands of years, with a wide margin.

content.myth: The largest organism has to be a whale or a giant tree.
content.reality: By ground covered it is a fungus you would walk past without noticing, and by weight the record goes to a clone of aspen. The biggest living things are not the ones we picture.
The mushrooms are only its fruiting bodies, the short-lived parts that release spores. The organism is the sprawling, mostly hidden mesh of threads and cords in the soil.

content.myth: It is the oldest living thing on Earth.
content.reality: Its age is a rough estimate, and it is not the oldest. Other clonal organisms, from a Utah aspen to Mediterranean seagrass, carry age estimates that reach much further back.

content.label: Ferguson BA et al. Coarse-scale population structure of pathogenic Armillaria species in a mixed-conifer forest in the Blue Mountains of northeast Oregon. Can J For Res 2003;33:612-623
Smith ML, Bruhn JN, Anderson JB. The fungus Armillaria bulbosa is among the largest and oldest living organisms. Nature 1992;356:428-431
Strange but True: The Largest Organism on Earth Is a Fungus (Scientific American)
